Class GlobalExceptionHandler

java.lang.Object
de.hallerweb.enterprise.prioritize.controller.GlobalExceptionHandler

    • handleNotFound

    • handleConflict

    • handleDataIntegrityViolation

      @ExceptionHandler(org.springframework.dao.DataIntegrityViolationException.class) public org.springframework.http.ResponseEntity<ApiError> handleDataIntegrityViolation(org.springframework.dao.DataIntegrityViolationException ex)
      A rejected constraint (a foreign key still pointing at the row being deleted, a unique violation) is a conflict with the current data — not a permission problem. Without this mapping such a failure escapes unhandled and Spring Security's translation filter turns it into a misleading 403, which is what made the "cannot delete a project that still has task schedules" case so hard to read. The database message is not echoed back; it belongs in the log, not in the response.

    • handleUnreadableBody

      @ExceptionHandler(org.springframework.http.converter.HttpMessageNotReadableException.class) public org.springframework.http.ResponseEntity<ApiError> handleUnreadableBody(org.springframework.http.converter.HttpMessageNotReadableException ex)
      A body that cannot be deserialized is a client mistake, so it must read as 400. Without this mapping Spring's default resolver handles it outside the advice, the error dispatch is rejected by the security chain, and the caller sees a 403 instead — which has now bitten twice in practice: omitting maxManDays when creating a project, and omitting active when creating a user. Both are the same root cause, a missing JSON value for a primitive field.

      The parser's own message is not echoed back: it names internal classes and fields. It goes to the log, where it is exactly what one needs while debugging a request.

    • handleAmbiguousData

      @ExceptionHandler(org.springframework.dao.IncorrectResultSizeDataAccessException.class) public org.springframework.http.ResponseEntity<ApiError> handleAmbiguousData(org.springframework.dao.IncorrectResultSizeDataAccessException ex)
      A lookup that must yield a single row found several. That is not the caller's fault and not a permission problem — it is inconsistent stored data, so it answers 500 and is logged at error level with the query's own message.

      The realistic case is a database written before usernames became unique: two accounts share a name, and every lookup by that name — including the login path — fails from then on. New duplicates are rejected in UserService, but existing rows have to be cleaned up by hand, and until then this at least says so instead of showing a 403.

    • handleSlotNotReserved

      @ExceptionHandler(SlotNotReservedException.class) public org.springframework.http.ResponseEntity<ApiError> handleSlotNotReserved(SlotNotReservedException ex)

      @ExceptionHandler(ResourceOfflineException.class) public org.springframework.http.ResponseEntity<ApiError> handleResourceOffline(ResourceOfflineException ex)
